mcqs.question: Which class is at the root of the C++ stream class hierarchy?
mcqs.explanation: ios_base is the root/base class providing platform-independent interface. It contains format flags, locale settings, and stream state information used by all stream classes.

mcqs.question: What type of stream is std::cout?
mcqs.explanation: cout is an ostream object (specifically a basic_ostream<char>) connected to standard output. It only supports output operations, not input.

mcqs.question: Which file stream class is used to open a file for both reading and writing?
mcqs.explanation: fstream (file stream) provides both input and output capabilities as it inherits from iostream. It supports ios::in | ios::out modes for bidirectional operations.

mcqs.question: What happens when you open a file with ios::trunc mode?

options.text: Content is preserved and new content is appended
Existing content is discarded if file exists
File is opened for reading only
File is opened in binary mode

mcqs.explanation: ios::trunc (truncate) discards existing content in the file. If the file exists, its previous content is deleted and its length becomes 0. This is the default for ofstream.

mcqs.question: Which member function is used to check if a stream operation has failed?
mcqs.explanation: fail() returns true when the failbit is set, indicating a logical error (like type mismatch during extraction). bad() indicates fatal error, eof() indicates end-of-file reached, and good() indicates all is well.

mcqs.question: What is the correct way to open a file for appending in binary mode?
mcqs.explanation: For append + binary: ios::app positions at end for writes, ios::binary prevents newline translation. Combining both gives append in binary mode.

mcqs.question: What is the primary difference between cerr and clog?

options.text: cerr cannot be redirected, clog can
cerr is buffered, clog is unbuffered
cerr is unbuffered, clog is buffered

mcqs.explanation: cerr is unbuffered (output appears immediately), while clog is buffered. Both are connected to standard error, but buffering affects when output actually occurs.

mcqs.question: Which of these is NOT a valid file opening mode in C++?
mcqs.explanation: ios::create is not a valid mode. Valid modes include: ios::in, ios::out, ios::app, ios::ate, ios::trunc, ios::binary. Files are created automatically when opened for output if they don't exist.

mcqs.question: When does the eof() flag become true in C++ streams?

options.text: When the next read would fail due to EOF
Immediately after the last character is read
When an attempt is made to read past EOF
Before the last valid read operation

mcqs.explanation: eof() becomes true only AFTER an attempt to read PAST the end of file fails. It does NOT predict EOF - it indicates that a read operation has already failed due to reaching EOF.

mcqs.question:
What does the following code do?
std::fstream file("data.txt", ios::in | ios::out | ios::trunc);

options.text: Opens file for reading and appending
Creates new file, truncates if exists, for read/write
Opens existing file for reading only

mcqs.explanation: ios::trunc creates the file if it doesn't exist, and if it exists, truncates it to zero length. Combined with ios::in | ios::out, it opens for both reading and writing.

mcqs.question: Which stream class would you use to read from a string as if it were a stream?
mcqs.explanation: stringstream (or istringstream/ostringstream) is used to treat a string as a stream. It's part of <sstream> header and allows string manipulation using stream operators.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the streambuf class?

options.text: To manage character buffers for stream I/O
To handle file opening and closing

mcqs.explanation: streambuf is the buffer management class. It handles the physical storage of characters (the buffer) and provides get/put areas for reading/writing data efficiently.

mcqs.question: Which operator is used to put a single character into an output stream?
mcqs.explanation: The put() member function writes a single character to the output stream. The << operator is for formatted output, and write() is for unformatted block output.

mcqs.question: If file.open() fails, what will fail() return?
mcqs.explanation: If open() fails, the failbit is set, so fail() returns true. You should always check if the file opened successfully before performing operations.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between ios::ate and ios::app when opening a file?

options.text: ate positions at end initially but allows seeking elsewhere, app always writes at end
app allows seeking, ate does not
ate opens in text mode, app opens in binary mode

mcqs.explanation: ios::ate (at end) positions the file pointer at the end initially but allows repositioning anywhere in the file. ios::app (append) always forces writes to occur at the end of the file.
